questions.question_text: How often must Maryland real estate licensees renew their licenses?
distractors: Every year
Every 3 years
Every 4 years
questions.explanation: Maryland real estate licenses operate on a 2-year renewal cycle. Licensees must complete required continuing education and submit renewal applications before their license expires.

questions.question_text: How many total hours of continuing education must a Maryland salesperson complete per renewal cycle?
distractors: 12 hours
18 hours
24 hours
questions.explanation: Maryland requires 15 hours of continuing education per 2-year renewal cycle. This includes mandatory components for fair housing, legislative updates, and ethics.

questions.question_text: Starting October 2025, how many hours of fair housing education are required in each CE cycle?
distractors: 1 hour
3 hours
4 hours
questions.explanation: Effective October 1, 2025 (HB 972, Ch. 397), Maryland requires a 2-hour fair housing course (up from 1.5 hours) within the 15-hour CE requirement. Licensees who practice solely in commercial real estate instead complete a mandatory 2-hour federal ADA course.

questions.question_text: A salesperson completed only 10 of 15 required CE hours before her renewal deadline. What is the consequence?
distractors: She receives a 90-day grace period
She pays a late fee and continues practicing
Her license converts to inactive automatically
questions.explanation: Failure to complete all required CE hours by the renewal deadline results in license expiration. The licensee cannot practice until the education is completed and the license is properly renewed.

questions.question_text: A broker's CE requirements include a component not required of salespersons. What is this additional topic?
distractors: Advanced appraisal methods of 3 hours
Commercial leasing of 2 hours
Business planning of 1 hour
questions.explanation: Brokers — along with designated branch office managers and team leaders — must complete a 1.5-hour broker supervision course (Category I) as part of their 15 CE hours; it replaces elective hours rather than adding to the total. HB 972 (Ch. 397, 2025) reduced this course from 3 hours to 1.5 effective October 1, 2025.

questions.question_text: A salesperson's affiliation with her broker ends. What does Maryland law require regarding notice to the Commission?
questions.correct_answer: The broker must immediately notify the Commission in writing
distractors: The salesperson must notify the Commission within 10 days
The broker must notify the Commission within 30 days
The broker holds the salesperson's license until she finds a new broker
questions.explanation: Under BOP §17-320, when a salesperson's or associate broker's affiliation ends, the broker must immediately mail notice of the termination to the licensee, submit written notice to the Commission, and return the license certificate to the Commission. Maryland has no 10-day notification window.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to transfer her license to a new broker. What approval is needed?
questions.correct_answer: Her license must be reissued by the Commission under the new broker
distractors: Only written consent from both brokers
No approval needed if she notifies within 10 days
Approval from the Department of Labor
questions.explanation: A transfer is processed through the Commission: the licensee applies (via MREC's electronic licensing) with the new broker's commitment, and she may not provide brokerage services until her license is issued under the new broker. BOP §17-320 requires the former broker to notify the Commission and return the license certificate when the affiliation ends.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to take a break from real estate but keep her license. She places her license on inactive status. What limitations apply?
questions.correct_answer: She cannot practice and needs no broker affiliation
distractors: She can still close pending transactions
She must maintain a broker affiliation
She must complete CE but cannot list property
questions.explanation: Inactive status means the licensee cannot provide real estate brokerage services, and no broker affiliation is required while inactive. Under BOP §17-316, however, an inactive license expires 3 years after it is placed on inactive status unless reactivated (which requires a fee, catch-up CE, and — for salespersons and associate brokers — evidence of a commitment from a broker).

questions.question_text: A salesperson's broker unexpectedly closes the firm. The salesperson continues showing homes while searching for a new broker. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, she cannot practice without a broker affiliation
distractors: Yes, for up to 30 days while transitioning
Yes, if she notifies the MREC immediately
Only if she limits activity to existing clients
questions.explanation: A salesperson cannot practice real estate without an active broker affiliation. When the broker relationship ends, all licensed activity must stop until a new affiliation is established and approved.

questions.question_text: An associate broker on inactive status receives an offer to join a new firm. What must happen before she can practice?
questions.correct_answer: Reactivate the license and affiliate with a broker
distractors: Complete 60 hours of pre-license education again
Pass the state exam a second time
Obtain written approval from her former broker
questions.explanation: A licensee on inactive status cannot practice real estate. She must reactivate her license by meeting any outstanding CE requirements and then establish a new broker affiliation before resuming practice.

questions.question_text: A corporation, LLC, or partnership providing real estate brokerage services in Maryland must designate which type of licensee?
questions.correct_answer: A licensed real estate broker as the broker of the firm
distractors: Managing salesperson
Compliance officer
Office administrator
questions.explanation: Under BOP §17-321, a business entity provides brokerage services through a licensed real estate broker whom the entity designates as the broker of the firm; the broker must notify the Commission (name, address, trade names, branch offices, affiliated licensees) before services are provided. 'Qualifying broker' is other states' terminology.

questions.question_text: Three investors form a Maryland real estate brokerage LLC. Only one holds a real estate license. Is this ownership structure permissible?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, Maryland sets no minimum share of licensed owners
distractors: No, at least one-third of the owners must be licensed
No, a majority of the owners must hold real estate licenses
Yes, but only if the unlicensed investors are immediate family members
questions.explanation: Maryland imposes no minimum percentage of licensed owners for a brokerage entity. The only ownership restriction, BOP §17-511, is a cap: not more than 50% of the interest in the business may be held by associate real estate brokers and salespersons. The entity must provide brokerage services through a licensed broker designated as broker of the firm (§17-321).

questions.question_text: A Maryland broker runs her brokerage from a home office where she and her staff regularly transact business and keep the firm's records. Does this satisfy the office requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, an office where business is regularly transacted complies
distractors: No, a home may never serve as a broker's office
No, the office must be located in a commercial zone
Only if no client meetings occur at that location
questions.explanation: BOP §17-517 requires each resident broker to maintain an office in the State, and COMAR 09.11.01.11 defines it as an office or headquarters where the broker and employees regularly transact brokerage business and where records are maintained. An answering service, recording device, or mail drop does not qualify — but nothing bars a bona fide home office or requires public accessibility.

questions.question_text: A broker opens a second office location in another county. What must she do regarding this branch?
questions.correct_answer: Register the branch office separately with the MREC
distractors: Nothing, if a licensed agent staffs the location
Obtain a separate broker license for that county
File a zoning application with the county
questions.explanation: Maryland requires each branch office to be separately registered with the MREC. This ensures the Commission knows about and can oversee all locations where licensed activity occurs.

questions.question_text: A corporation with six shareholders wants to provide real estate brokerage services in Maryland. One shareholder is a licensed broker; the rest are unlicensed investors. May it do so?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if services are provided through the designated broker of the firm
distractors: No, at least one-third of the shareholders must be licensed
No, Maryland requires the corporation itself to hold a brokerage license
Only if each unlicensed shareholder owns less than 10%
questions.explanation: Maryland has no minimum licensed-ownership requirement for brokerage entities and does not license the entity itself. BOP §17-511 only caps ownership by associate brokers and salespersons at 50% of the interest in the business, and §17-321 requires the entity to designate a licensed broker as the broker of the firm, who notifies the Commission before services are provided. Unlicensed investors may own shares.
